stem: A 29-year-old man with schizophrenia is still floridly psychotic after two successive antipsychotic trials, each at an adequate dose for 8 weeks with pharmacy-confirmed adherence. His team wants the single agent with proven superiority for this presentation and accepts that it obligates registry-enforced blood-count monitoring. Which antipsychotic is indicated?
explanation: Two failed adequate antipsychotic trials define treatment-resistant schizophrenia, and clozapine is the ONLY antipsychotic proven superior in this exact scenario (and the only one shown to reduce suicidality). Because of agranulocytosis it uniquely mandates registry ANC monitoring — weekly for 6 months, then every 2 weeks to 12 months, then every 4 weeks. Olanzapine, risperidone and quetiapine are all reasonable general choices but none is a treatment-resistance-specific agent and none carries mandatory blood-count surveillance; selecting one here simply repeats a strategy that has already failed.

source.name: Clozapine monograph (boxed warning; ANC monitoring schedule)

stem: A 22-year-old man with a first episode of schizophrenia already has obesity, impaired fasting glucose and a strong family history of type 2 diabetes. His psychiatrist wants the antipsychotic least likely to worsen his weight, glucose and lipids. Which is the best metabolic choice among these options?
explanation: Lurasidone has minimal H1 and muscarinic activity, making it weight-, lipid- and glucose-favourable with low prolactin — the metabolically safest of these four. Olanzapine is among the two worst SGAs metabolically (marked weight gain, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia/DKA), quetiapine carries moderate metabolic risk, and risperidone is intermediate. In a metabolically vulnerable patient lurasidone best protects weight and glucose (ziprasidone and aripiprazole would be comparably favourable but are not offered here).

stem: A 41-year-old woman with schizophrenia needs an antipsychotic, but her baseline ECG shows a QTc of 480 ms and she is also maintained on methadone; her electrolytes are being optimized. The team wants to AVOID the agent most likely to prolong her QT interval further. Which antipsychotic should be avoided here?
explanation: Ziprasidone produces the greatest QT prolongation of the SGAs — its defining caution, with torsades risk that mandates avoiding other QT-prolonging drugs and correcting electrolytes — so it is exactly the wrong choice in a patient with QTc 480 ms on methadone. Aripiprazole and brexpiprazole have no notable QT signal, and lurasidone is low-QT risk; all three are far safer cardiac choices here. The discrimination is recognizing ziprasidone as the QT outlier.

source.name: Ziprasidone monograph (greatest QT prolongation of the SGAs)

stem: A 34-year-old man with schizophrenia has well-controlled positive symptoms but disabling, persistent negative symptoms — flat affect, anhedonia and amotivation — despite good adherence. His psychiatrist wants the antipsychotic whose D3-preferring partial agonism is specifically theorized to benefit negative symptoms. Which agent best fits?
explanation: Cariprazine is the most D3-selective antipsychotic — a D3-preferring D2/D3 partial agonist whose D3 preference is theorized to benefit negative symptoms and anhedonia. Aripiprazole and brexpiprazole are also D2/D3 partial agonists, but neither is D3-preferring. Risperidone is a full D2 antagonist — in fact the most D2-potent SGA, with high EPS and hyperprolactinemia — and offers no negative-symptom advantage. The D3 preference is what singles out cariprazine among the partial agonists.

stem: A 24-year-old man with schizophrenia needs a first-generation antipsychotic but has already had two acute dystonic reactions and severe akathisia on previous agents. The team is willing to accept sedation and orthostasis in exchange for the lowest possible risk of further extrapyramidal reactions. Which first-generation antipsychotic has the lowest extrapyramidal liability?
explanation: Chlorpromazine is the prototype LOW-potency FGA: its strong H1, muscarinic and α1 blockade brings sedation, anticholinergic effects and orthostasis but comparatively LESS EPS than high-potency agents — the right trade-off for an EPS-prone patient. Haloperidol and fluphenazine are high-potency agents with maximal EPS and minimal sedation, precisely what caused this patient's dystonia and akathisia. Perphenazine is mid-potency with intermediate (still greater than chlorpromazine) EPS. Lower potency buys the least extrapyramidal risk at the cost of autonomic burden.

source.name: First-generation antipsychotic potency comparison (EPS vs autonomic burden)
